data.title: U.K. Spending Cuts Will Be Politically Challenging to Implement — Market Talk
data.description: The U.K. government will face political challenges in implementing potential spending cuts it is due to outline in its budget on Nov. 26, Bank of America economists say in a note. Any cuts would be hard to deliver given tight government spending plans and the tough department negotiations at the Treasury's spending review in June, the economists say.(miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: Tesla Stock Edges Higher as Reviews of 'New' EVs Tickle In — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

Tesla has new models to entice car buyers. Now car reviewers are driving them. The vehicles perform well, but ride quality isn't the biggest question facing investors.

On Tuesday, Tesla unveiled "Standard" lower-priced versions of the Model 3 and Model Y, starting at about $37,000 and $40,000, respectively. The new vehicles have fewer features and a shorter per-charge range than most of the "Premium" versions of the cars. Automotive data provider Edmunds was given early access to the pair and took them for a spin.

"The new variant of the Model Y is the big surprise," wrote Alistair Weaver, Edmunds editor in chief . It lacks "sporty reflexes" but is still a comfortable ride. "For only a $5,000 price difference, questions remain about whether the new Standard trim is a good value or just slightly cheaper."

That's the main issue for investors. Will a gap of a few thousand dollars be enough to increase the pool of Tesla buyers? Or will shoppers, already likely to buy a Tesla, trade down to save some money?

It's too early to tell, but the new Model 3 and Y look like solid offerings that feel like traditional Teslas, said Edmunds.

"The new Standard Model 3 offers fewer compromises in driving quality than the new Model Y, maintaining strong performance and sporty handling, " added Weaver. "In contrast to the Model Y, the Standard version of the Model 3 retains more value with its price drop from the Premium trim."

Investors, who expected the new models, appear fine with the vehicles. Coming into Friday trading, Tesla stock was up about 1.3%. The reaction to the new cars, overall, has been small.

Tesla reports third-quarter results on Oct. 22. Investors will want to hear about the new vehicles and order activity then.

Tesla stock was rising 0.4% in premarket trading at $437.22, while S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average futures were up 0.1%.

Coming into Friday trading, Tesla stock was up about 8% so far this year and 82% over the past 12 months. The gains have come despite falling car sales. Investors are more focused on Tesla's AI opportunities, such as the robo-taxi business it launched in Austin, Texas, in June.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: REG - Schroder Inc Growth - Compliance with Market Abuse Regulation
data.description:
RNS Number : 9733C Schroder Income Growth Fund PLC 10 October 2025  

10 October 2025

SCHRODER INCOME GROWTH FUND PLC

(the "Company")

Compliance with the Market Abuse Regulation ("MAR")

The Company announces that it is satisfied that all inside information which the Directors and the Company may have in the period leading up to the announcement of its full year results for the year ended 31 August 2025 has previously been, and will continue to be, notified via a regulatory information service ("RIS"). Therefore, the Company is not prohibited from dealing in its own securities.

The mandatory closed period under MAR will last until the full year results in respect of the year ended 31 August 2025 are published and is anticipated to end no sooner than 12 November 2025.

If, in the period leading up to the announcement of the full year results, the Directors of the Company come into the possession of any inside information, this will be notified to an RIS before any such transactions are undertaken.

Enquiries:

Schroder Investment Management Limited

Francesca Davis / Natalia de Sousa

Tel: 020 7658 6000

data.title: REG - CPPGroup PLC - Director/PDMR Shareholding
data.description:
RNS Number : 9732C CPPGroup PLC 10 October 2025  

CPPGroup Plc ("CPP", "CPP Group" or the "Company")

Exercise of Options, Total Voting Rights and Director/PDMR Shareholdings

Exercise of Options

CPPGroup Plc announces that, on 7 October 2025, Simon Pyper, Chief Financial Officer of the Company, exercised 109,971 options over ordinary shares of £1 each in the Company ("Ordinary Shares") under the CPPGroup Plc Deferred Bonus Plan (the "Options Exercise").

The Options Exercise is being satisfied by Ordinary Shares held by the Company's Employee Benefit Trust and from a new issue of Ordinary Shares. Those shares required to be issued are covered by the Company's existing block-listing application.

Total Voting Rights

The Company's issued share capital comprises 9,164,804 Ordinary Shares. Following the Options Exercise, the total voting rights of the Company will be 9,269,605. Therefore, the total number of voting rights in the Company is 9,269,605.

The above figure of 9,269,605 may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, the Company under the FCA's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules.

Director/PDMR shareholdings

Following the Options Exercise, Simon Pyper's beneficial interest in Ordinary Shares of the Company will be 212,001 Ordinary Shares, representing approximately 2.29% of the issued share capital of the Company.

CPPGroup plc   

Simon Pyper, Chief Financial Officer                        via Alma Strategic Communications        

Panmure Liberum

(Nominated Adviser and Sole Broker)                    Tel: +44 (0)20 3100 2000

CPP Group is a technology-driven assistance company that creates embedded and ancillary real-time assistance products and resolution services that reduce disruption to everyday life for millions of people across the world, at the time and place they are needed, CPP Group is listed on AIM, operated by the London Stock Exchange.

data.title: US 1-Year Inflation Expectations Slightly Lower in October
data.description:
The year-ahead inflation expectations in the United States, as compiled by the survey of the University of Michigan, eased for the second month to 4.6% in October 2025, from 4.7% in September, according to preliminary figures.

Meanwhile, the five-year inflation outlook stood at 3.7% in October, matching September's reading.

data.title: US UNIVERSITY OF MICHIGAN CONSUMER SENTIMENT: HIGH PRICES, WEAKENING JOB PROSPECTS AT FOREFRONT OF CONSUMER CONCERN, NO EXPECTATION OF IMPROVEMENT
Mysteries: Richard Osman's 'The Impossible Fortune'
data.description:
By Tom Nolan

A collective melancholy hovers at the start of Richard Osman's "The Impossible Fortune" (Pamela Dorman, 368 pages, $30), the fifth book starring the group of English senior sleuths known as the Thursday Murder Club. Elizabeth, the club's leader, struggles to cope in the wake of her husband's recent death. Ron, an ex-labor activist, feels old and weak. Joyce, a single mother, stresses over her long-grown daughter's impending wedding. And Ibrahim, an unmarried psychologist, is achingly lonely: "Murders are all well and good, but who does he have?"

The club gets a jolt when a man named Nick Silver approaches Elizabeth. He's sure someone is trying to murder him. Nick and his business partner run a security company that guards a bitcoin fortune with a six-digit code. It seems that multiple parties covet that code — maybe enough to kill for it. As soon as Elizabeth agrees to help, Nick drops from sight. Then his partner is blown up by a car bomb.

Elizabeth pushes the crew into action. Ron's participation is a bit halfhearted, though, since his daughter has recently fled her abusive and vengeful husband. Ibrahim's focus is also fractured. The felonious woman he's counseling ("You must give something back to society, Connie") has taken his advice — by mentoring a teenage girl into a life of crime.

"The Impossible Fortune," like its series predecessors, is an exhilarating work. Mr. Osman's irresistible characters are resolute in old age, even in the face of mortal danger. "There may be trouble ahead, " Ron reflects. "Bring it on."

It has been almost a decade since Ann Cleeves's last novel featuring the Scottish police detective Jimmy Perez. In "The Killing Stones" (Minotaur, 384 pages, $29), Jimmy has settled in Orkney with Willow Reeves, the professional and romantic partner with whom he has a young son. One stormy night on the island of Westray, at the site of an archaeological dig, the detective finds the battered body of his lifelong mate Archie Stout — his head bashed bloody by a Neolithic carved stone. As the pathologist at the scene observes: "This is a strange kind of do."

Jimmy struggles with his feelings as he investigates the death of "perhaps his closest friend ever." Willow, pregnant and officially on maternity leave, hastens to assist him in sifting through suspects. Archie was no saint. To his wife's disgust, he indulged in affairs. And he seemed to acquire as many enemies as allies.

A second murder occurs among the standing stones of Stenness, "Orkney's answer to Stonehenge." This victim has also been slain by a stone. When at last an explanation comes in this atmospheric and scenic procedural, it is as much of a shock to the reader as it is to Jimmy. "Things like this don't happen in Orkney," one citizen protests. Apparently they do, but it takes a soul as empathetic as Jimmy to figure out why.

"Picket Line" (Mariner, 128 pages, $22.99), a short work written in 1970 by Elmore Leonard (1925-2013), sees print for the first time with an illuminating introduction by the author's biographer C.M. Kushins. This "lost novella," a transitional work between Leonard's early Western stories and his later novels of cops and crooks, began as an outline for a proposed movie script. It grew into a story bearing the stylistic hallmarks of the novelist's later works.

Set in Trinity, Texas, during a strike among migrant melon pickers, the tale unfolds through the shifting viewpoints of several figures. These include Vincent Mora, a seasoned union leader; Paco Rojas and Chino de la Cruz, a pair of travelers on an ambiguous mission; and Connie Chavez, an activist whose bullhorned taunts stir up the strikers.

There is physical violence in "Picket Line," but no central crime to be solved, only a struggle for social justice. Suspense springs from guessing who will stick with the fight and who might abandon or sell it out. "Once a man walks out of the field and joins a picket line," Vincent declares, "he's never the same again." With this surprisingly mature work, Mr. Kushins writes, "Leonard drew his own line in the sand."

data.title: First direct Riyadh–Moscow flight operated by Saudia lands in Russian capital amid warming ties
data.description:
The first direct passenger flight from Riyadh to Moscow operated by Saudia, Saudi Arabia's national airline, touched down in the Russian capital on Friday amid warming ties between the two oil producers.

Saudia said it is planning three direct return flights per week to Moscow's Sheremetyevo airport in a move likely to spur travel in both directions from visitors, business people and government officials.

Russia has already seen an increase in tourists from the kingdom, and Flynas, a Saudi budget airline, launched three direct flights a week between Riyadh and Moscow's Vnukovo airport in August.

Flynas is also due to launch a direct route between Jeddah, the gateway for pilgrims visiting Mecca, and Moscow in December.

Ties between Russia and Saudi Arabia are close.

Russian President Vladimir Putin last year thanked Saudi Arabia's Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman for helping to organise the biggest U.S.-Russian prisoner swap since the Cold War.

Putin and Mohammed bin Salman, also known as MbS, have fostered a close personal relationship since 2015 when the prince visited Russia for the first time.

The relationship has helped the leaders of the world's two biggest oil exporters conclude and maintain the OPEC+ energy deal.

data.title: BMO lifts Prologis PT on steady growth despite higher vacancies
data.description:
** BMO raises PT on industrial REIT Prologis to $105 from $95, maintains rating at "underperform"

** New PT shows a downside of 8.3% from the stock's last close

** BMO sees Prologis well-positioned for steady growth, supported by strong leasing activity, solid occupancy and disciplined development plans

** Brokerage says U.S. industrial vacancy has hit 7.5% in Q3 and is expected to peak at 8% by late 2026, while net absorption is projected to stay below new supply next year

** 16 of 24 brokerages rate the stock "buy" or higher, 7 "hold" and 1 "sell"; their median PT is $120 - data compiled by LSEG

** Including session moves, shares up 8.5% YTD

data.title: Venture Global sinks as BP's arbitration win renews worries over pending disputes
data.description:
By Vallari Srivastava

Venture Global shares plunged about 20% on Friday after the U.S. LNG exporter lost an arbitration with BP BP.L over failure to deliver on a long-term gas supply contract, stoking worries of similar verdicts in pending disputes with other customers seeking billions of dollars in compensation.

The ruling in BP's favour came as a surprise after Venture Global's recent arbitration win against Shell .

BP is seeking more than $1 billion in damages plus interest and legal costs, with the final amount to be determined in a separate hearing in 2026.

Venture Global, the second-largest U.S. liquefied natural gas supplier, is facing other arbitration claims with customers seeking compensation of more than $4 billion linked to the delayed commercial start-up of the Calcasieu Pass project, according to the company's 2024 annual report.

The company previously warned that losing current or future arbitration cases could result in significant financial penalties, the termination of some gas supply contracts, and accelerated repayment of project debt.

All of the companies that have filed arbitration are seeking compensation, and none of them have sought to terminate their contracts in their proceedings, Venture has said.

It is unclear why the result of the BP arbitration was different to the Shell case, and whether this was due to differences in contracts or in the company's legal strategies.

Gabriel Moreen of Mizuho Securities called the ruling a "negative surprise," saying it introduces uncertainty for the remaining claims.

Friday's drop looked set to wipe out nearly $6 billion from the company's market valuation, if current losses hold. Since its January IPO, the shares have fallen more than 58%.

The stock declines also reflect ongoing investor caution over LNG commissioning risks.

Venture Global is expanding its production amid surging global demand, with the U.S. becoming the world's top LNG supplier in 2023.

CALCASIEU PROJECT IN SPOTLIGHT

In addition to BP and Shell , Italy's Edison , Portugal's Galp , Spain's Repsol , and Poland's Orlen have filed arbitration claims accusing the U.S. company of profiteering by selling LNG cargoes at higher spot market prices, rather than delivering them under long-term contracts.

Edison and Repsol told Reuters they were still pursuing their arbitration cases against Venture Global. Orlen declined to comment but said it remains in arbitration with the U.S. company.

Venture Global claimed that the Calcasieu Pass was still starting up, and had not reached the status of commercial operations. Long-term contracts do not take effect until the plant reaches commercial operations.

Citi said the result was "unexpected," adding that the tighter language in BP's contract likely contributed to the outcome.

The International Chamber of Commerce International Court of Arbitration found that it had breached its obligations to declare commercial operations had begun at the Calcasieu Pass plant in a timely manner and act as a "reasonable and prudent operator," Venture Global said on Thursday.

The company said it is evaluating all options and will continue to vigorously defend its position.
